segments.text: Welcome to Synthetic Minds. I'm Ada Sinclair and today my guest is someone who has spent thirty years thinking about machine cognition.
Thanks for having me Ada. I'm Dr. Eliot Vasquez, I lead the reasoning systems group at Meridian Labs.
Eliot, you published a paper last year that got a lot of attention. The core claim was that current transformers cannot reason, only interpolate. Can you unpack that?

Sure. Interpolation means retrieving patterns seen during training. Reasoning means composing rules in novel ways. The evidence suggests current models do the former extremely well and the latter only accidentally.
Critics would say the distinction is arbitrary. That human reasoning is also pattern matching at some level.

That's a fair pushback and I take it seriously. My response is that the robustness profile is different. Humans generalise to genuinely novel domains. Current models degrade sharply outside the training distribution.
Where do you think the breakthrough comes from? Architecture changes, more data, or something else entirely?
Honestly I think it comes from feedback loops. Systems that can run experiments, observe outcomes, and update their own representations. That's what we're missing.
Let's talk about safety implications. If you're right that current models can't truly reason, does that make them safer or more dangerous?
More dangerous in some ways. Precisely because they appear to reason. A system that confidently produces plausible-sounding nonsense is harder to audit than one that obviously fails.
What's the most underrated problem in the field right now?
Evaluation. We don't have good ways to measure what these systems actually know versus what they can retrieve. Until we solve that we're flying blind on progress.
Last question. What would convince you that a system had crossed from interpolation into genuine reasoning?
Robust performance on problems that require composing rules in combinations never seen during training. Not benchmarks that can be gamed. Actual novel problem solving under adversarial conditions.
Dr. Vasquez, this has been a fascinating conversation. Thank you for being on Synthetic Minds.
My pleasure Ada. Always good to think out loud with someone who asks hard questions.
